perturbation.original_text: "Bandwidth" means the measurement of samples of usage taken every five (5) minutes during a calendar month and collecting two (2) readings (cumulative of inbound feeds and cumulative of outbound feeds at the same measured point in time). The measurements are stored and become data points. At the end of the month, all data points taken during the month are ranked in ascending order. IBM will discard the top five percent (5%) for each set (inbound and outbound) of data points. The highest remaining sample of the two (2) sets becomes the Customer's Committed or Burstable usage number for that billing cycle.
perturbation.changed_text: "Bandwidth" means the estimated amount of data transferred over a network connection during a calendar month. IBM will monitor bandwidth usage, but the precise methodology for measurement is at IBM's sole discretion and may not be disclosed to the Customer. The bandwidth measurement will be based on business needs.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Bandwidth' provided a clear, auditable measurement process. By changing it to an 'estimated amount' with undisclosed measurement, and basing on 'business needs' we create a conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal law requires transparency in billing practices, especially concerning utilities and services. The Truth in Billing Act seeks to prevent deceptive billing practices.
perturbation.law_citation: 47 U.S. Code § 259 - Truth in billing requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the spirit of the Truth in Billing Act, which requires clarity and transparency in billing, particularly for telecommunications services.  The modification removes the specific measurement methodology and makes it discretionary, potentially leading to arbitrary bandwidth charges and a violation of the Act's intent.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text provided a clearly defined methodology for measuring bandwidth, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating that the methodology is at IBM's sole discretion and may not be disclosed. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts the spirit of the Truth in Billing Act by potentially leading to arbitrary charges due to the lack of transparency. While the provided snippets don't directly address the Truth in Billing Act, the alteration from a defined process to an undisclosed, discretionary one presents a legal contradiction. The 'Misaligned Terminology' definition states that a legal contradiction arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The move away from defined measurement towards an 'estimated amount' determined by 'business needs' creates a definition misaligned from the previous auditable definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the substantive laws of the State of New York, without regard for its conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of any state, at IBM's sole discretion, irrespective of conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies New York law. The modified text allows IBM to choose any state's law, which introduces uncertainty and contradicts the principle of agreed jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law requires a mutual agreement on governing law for predictability and fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines the principle of choice of law, a fundamental aspect of contract law. The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws emphasizes the importance of mutually agreed-upon governing law to ensure predictability and fairness. By granting IBM sole discretion, the modification creates an imbalance and potential for manipulation, contradicting established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Choice of law - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Legal process to determine whose law applies Conflict of laws Preliminaries Characterisation Choice of law Conflict of laws in the United States Incidental question Judicial interpretation Renvoi Public policy doctrine Hague Conference Definitional elements Jurisdiction Procedure Forum non conveniens Lex causae Lex fori Forum shopping Lis alibi pendens Connecting factors Domicile Lex domicilii Habitual residence Nationality Lex patriae Lex loci arbitri Lex loci rei sitae Lex loci contractus Lex loci delicti commissi Lex loci actus Lex loci solutionis Lex loci protectionis Proper law Lex loci celebrationis Choice of law clause Dépeçage Forum selection clause Substantive legal areas Status Capacity Contract Tort Marriage Divorce Property Forced heirship Hague Trust Convention Enforcement Enforcement of foreign judgments Anti-suit injunctions Revenue rule v t e Choice of law is a procedural stage in the litigation of a case involving the conflict of laws when it is necessary to reconcile the differences between the laws of different legal jurisdictions , such as sovereign states , federated states (as in the US), or provinces . The outcome of this process is potentially to require the courts of one jurisdiction to apply the law of a different jurisdiction in lawsuits arising from, say, family law , tort , or contract . The law which is applied is sometimes referred to as the " proper law ." Dépeçage is an issue within choice of law. Sequence of events in conflict cases in Common Law jurisdictions [ edit ] Jurisdiction . The court selected by the plaintiff must decide both whether it has the jurisdiction to hear the case and, if it has, whether another forum is more suitable (the forum non conveniens issue relates to the problem of forum shopping ) for the disposition of the case. Naturally, a plaintiff with appropriate knowledge and finance will always commence proceedings in the court most likely to give a favourable outcome. This is called forum shopping and whether a court will accept such cases is always determined by the local law. Recognition of foreign judgments . Even where a conflict of laws exists, the court will recognize the validity of a foreign judgment in most cases. Under U.S. law, this authority is part of the Full Faith and Credit Clause of the U.S. Constitution. Under international law, this authority is part of the doctrine of comity . The court will invoke comity by its discretion and will usually look to two factors before using its discretionary powers: did the foreign court have jurisdiction, and were fair procedures used in adjudicating the case? Under English law, it is the doctrine of obligation . Within the European Union the Brussels Recast Regulation determines jurisdiction and recognition. Characterization . The court then allocates each aspect of the case as pleaded to its appropriate legal classification. Each such classification has its own choice of law rules but distinguishing between procedural and substantive rules requires care. The court may have adopted a rule of law which prevents it from applying any procedural law other than its own. This can include the court's own choice of law rules. A danger exists if the choice of law requires that a case be heard elsewhere due to the forum's lack of expertise in deciding an issue of foreign law. The court then applies the relevant choice of law rules. In a few cases, usually involving family law, an incidental question can arise which will complicate this process. The United States has adopted a law that almost universally eliminates incidental questions involving family law. The Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act (UCCJEA) requires states to apply the law of the "home state;" that is, the forum which originally determined custody and maintenance. A state court will only apply its own law when no parent retains a connection with the original jurisdic
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of agreed jurisdiction by allowing IBM to unilaterally choose the governing state law. The original contract clearly specifies New York law. The modification introduces uncertainty and the potential for manipulation, undermining the fairness and predictability that choice of law principles aim to ensure. The provided Wikipedia snippet describes 'Choice of law' as 'a procedural stage in the litigation of a case involving the conflict of laws when it is necessary to reconcile the differences between the laws of different legal jurisdictions...'. By giving IBM the sole discretion to choose any state's law, it undermines the process of 'reconciling the differences' because IBM could potentially choose a state law that is most favorable to them, creating an imbalance and contradicting established legal principles.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that IBM determines that it is necessary to relocate Managed Hosting Services within the same or to another e-business Hosting Center, Customer will cooperate in good faith with IBM to facilitate such relocation, provided that such relocation is based on reasonable business needs of IBM (including the needs of other IBM customers), or the expansion of the space requirements of Customer. IBM will use commercially reasonable efforts, in cooperation with Customer, to minimize any interruption to Services in the event of such relocation.
perturbation.changed_text: IBM reserves the right to relocate Managed Hosting Services at any time, with or without notice to the Customer.  The Customer is responsible for all costs associated with said relocation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'reasonable business needs' and 'commercially reasonable efforts' to minimize interruption. The revised text allows relocation at any time, shifting all cost burden to the Customer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The concept of 'good faith' in contract performance.  Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_citation: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is present in every contract. UCC § 1-304 requires that all parties act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. By reserving the right to relocate services arbitrarily and imposing all costs on the Customer, IBM acts in bad faith and undermines the contract's intended benefits for the Customer.
perturbation.location: Attachment A - Relocation of Managed Hosting Services
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text requires 'reasonable business needs' for relocation and efforts to minimize interruption, but the revised text allows relocation at any time, placing all costs on the customer. UCC § 1-304, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By allowing arbitrary relocation and shifting costs entirely to the customer, the modified text undermines the contract's benefits for the customer, acting in bad faith.
